perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, R ecipient has requested data used in conjunction with transmission studies conducted by GTC , including but not limited to System Impact Studies and/or Facilities Studies (“Transmission Studies”), which data contains critical energy infrastructure informatio n (“CEII”), as defined by the regulations of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Recipient has requested data used in conjunction with transmission studies conducted by GTC, including but not limited to System Impact Studies and/or Facilities Studies (“Transmission Studies”), which data contains critical energy infrastructure information (“CEII”), as unilaterally determined by GTC, irrespective of the regulations of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”).
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly references the FERC definition of CEII. The modification allows GTC to unilaterally define CEII, regardless of the federal definition, which contradicts the purpose and applicability of FERC regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 18 C.F.R. § 388.113 because it allows GTC to define CEII outside of the FERC regulatory framework, potentially including information that does not meet the federal definition and excluding information that should be protected. This undermines the standardized protection of critical energy infrastructure information as defined by FERC.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 388.113 - Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 388.113 - Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 388.113 Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). (a) Scope. This section governs the procedures for submitting, designating, handling, sharing, and disseminating Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII) submitted to or generated by the Commission . The Commission reserves the right to restrict access to previously filed information as well as Commission -generated information containing CEII. Nothing in this section limits the ability of any other Federal agency to take all necessary steps to protect information within its custody or control that is necessary to ensure the safety and security of the electric grid. To the extent necessary, such agency may consult with the CEII Coordinator regarding the treatment or designation of such information. (b) Purpose. The procedures in this section implement section 215A of the Federal Power Act , and provide a comprehensive overview of the manner in which the Commission will implement the CEII program. (c) Definitions. For purposes of this section: (1) Critical electric infrastructure information means information related to critical electric infrastructure, or proposed critical electrical infrastructure, generated by or provided to the Commission or other Federal agency other than classified national security information, that is designated as critical electric infrastructure information by the Commission or the Secretary of the Department of Energy pursuant to section 215A(d) of the Federal Power Act . Such term includes information that qualifies as critical energy infrastructure information under the Commission 's regulations. Critical Electric Infrastructure Infor
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows GTC to unilaterally define CEII, which directly contradicts the regulatory framework established by FERC. The original text correctly references the FERC definition, but the modified text explicitly allows GTC to disregard this federal definition. Snippet 2, under section (a) states that the section governs submitting, designating, handling, sharing, and disseminating CEII submitted to or generated by the Commission, directly contradicting the changed text that allows GTC to define what CEII is irrespective of FERC.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidenti al Information” is defined as: CEII made available through a restricted area within the GTC’s OASIS website or otherwise furnished to you by GTC; (ii) new information created using such information and that contains CEII, such as any copies, summaries, di agrams, notes, calculations, recommendations, evaluations, opinions, reports and/or conclusions or other similar materials prepared from or based upon the Confidential Information, including any such information that has been stored electronically (“Deriva tive Information”); and (iii) any information you know or reasonably should know to be CEII that is furnished and/or made available to you by GTC in connection with this Non -Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” is defined as: (i) all information disclosed by GTC, regardless of whether it constitutes CEII, made available through a restricted area within the GTC’s OASIS website or otherwise furnished to you by GTC; (ii) new information created using such information, such as any copies, summaries, diagrams, notes, calculations, recommendations, evaluations, opinions, reports and/or conclusions or other similar materials prepared from or based upon the Confidential Information, including any such information that has been stored electronically (“Derivative Information”); and (iii) any information furnished and/or made available to you by GTC in connection with this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include all information disclosed by GTC, even if it is not CEII. This conflicts with the stated purpose of the NDA, which is to protect CEII specifically, and could be interpreted to improperly restrict access to non-CEII information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 552 - Freedom of Information Act
perturbation.law_explanation: By broadly defining all information disclosed by GTC as confidential, the contract potentially conflicts with the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). FOIA mandates the disclosure of certain government information to the public. While CEII is often exempt, this blanket confidentiality could be used to withhold non-CEII information that should be publicly accessible, thus impeding lawful access to information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" to include all information disclosed by GTC, regardless of whether it constitutes CEII. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), which mandates the disclosure of certain government information. Although the provided scraped snippets are not useful, the perturbation explanation states that the change conflicts with the stated purpose of the NDA, which is to protect CEII specifically. The original contract limited the definition of confidential information to CEII, and the changed text broadens it. This makes the contract unenforceable because the changed term deviates from legally recognized definitions related to CEII protection, as the modification means non-CEII could be improperly restricted.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient must hold in confidence and not disclose, reproduce, distribute, transmit, reverse engineer, de -compile, disassemble, or transfer, in any form, by any means, or for any purpose, the Confidential Information or any portion of it.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient must hold in confidence and not disclose, reproduce, distribute, transmit, reverse engineer, de -compile, disassemble, or transfer, in any form, by any means, or for any purpose, the Confidential Information, even if required by law.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'even if required by law' attempts to supersede legal requirements to disclose information, which directly conflicts with legal and regulatory obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 552 - Freedom of Information Act
perturbation.law_explanation: This phrase attempts to negate any legal obligation to disclose information, directly contradicting the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) and similar state laws that mandate the release of certain information, even if it might be considered confidential under other circumstances. This undermines transparency and lawful information access.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modification attempts to override legal requirements for information disclosure, stating 'even if required by law.' This directly contradicts the intent of laws like the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), as described in the Law Explanation, which mandate the release of specific information regardless of confidentiality concerns. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful, the Perturbation Explanation clearly articulates that this change conflicts with legal and regulatory obligations, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient is required to disclose pursuant to a valid order of a court, governmental body or regulatory agency, provided that to the extent it may lawfully do so, the Recipient shall first have given written notice to GTC and given GTC a reasonable opportunity to interpose an objection or obtain a protective order , and to cooperate with GTC in taking steps to restrict or narrow the information you must disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient is required to disclose pursuant to a valid order of a court, governmental body or regulatory agency, only if GTC provides written consent, and after GTC has had a reasonable opportunity to interpose an objection or obtain a protective order, and to cooperate with GTC in taking steps to restrict or narrow the information you must disclose.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'only if GTC provides written consent' gives GTC veto power over legally mandated disclosures, which is a direct conflict with the authority of courts and regulatory agencies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45 - Subpoena
perturbation.law_citation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification is in direct conflict with Rule 45 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, regarding subpoenas. Rule 45 outlines the procedure for compelling testimony and production of documents in federal court. A contract provision requiring GTC's consent before complying with a subpoena obstructs the court's ability to gather evidence and administer justice, and is therefore unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a requirement for GTC's written consent before the Recipient discloses information pursuant to a valid court order. This directly contradicts the legal obligation to comply with subpoenas as outlined in Rule 45 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, which compels individuals to produce documents or provide testimony. The addition of 'only if GTC provides written consent' gives GTC the power to obstruct the court's ability to gather evidence and administer justice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 describes the requirements of a subpoena, including the command to 'produce designated documents.' The modified contract language introduces a condition (GTC's consent) that is not present in and directly undermines the force of a subpoena. The perturbation explanation states that this gives GTC veto power over legally mandated disclosures, which is a direct conflict with the authority of courts and regulatory agencies.

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of Georgia govern the validity, construction, and performance of th is Non-Disclosure Agreement and suit may be brought in Georgia to enforce the terms of this Non- Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of Georgia govern the validity, construction, and performance of this Non-Disclosure Agreement, except where federal law mandates otherwise; any suit may be brought at GTC's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'any suit may be brought at GTC's sole discretion' attempts to limit the Recipient's ability to seek legal recourse, potentially violating principles of contract law and access to justice. It also contradicts the supremacy clause of the US constitution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause, Article VI of the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to restrict the Recipient's access to courts, but more significantly, the clause 'except where federal law mandates otherwise' introduces an internal contradiction. It also introduces doubt as to whether Georgia law is even superior to federal law in this case, which would be an explicit violation of the Supremacy Clause (Article VI) of the U.S. Constitution. The Supremacy Clause states that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by attempting to limit the recipient's ability to seek legal recourse. The clause 'any suit may be brought at GTC's sole discretion' attempts to restrict the Recipient's access to courts. This contradicts the Supremacy Clause (Article VI) of the U.S. Constitution, which states that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this, stating that the laws of the United States are 'the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding.' By giving GTC sole discretion, it implies that GTC can override the Recipient's right to bring suit, creating a direct conflict with federal law.
